text: This book examines the EU’s efforts to reform the regime of international investment law and their impact on the rule of law and legitimacy of international investment law as a legal discipline. In three parts, the volume assembles a collection of in-depth research findings and critically assesses the different facets of EU’s reform initiatives. Part I offers a thorough overview of the EU reform approaches and explores their theoretical foundations, against the backdrop of EU constitutional and international law, as well as the rule of law and legitimacy in international investment law. Part II delves into the detail of the EU’s internal constitutional framework governing international investment law and its external investment policy in third countries, examining both the substantive and procedural dimensions of the EU’s reform agenda. Part III brings together thematic insights on the legitimacy of international investment law in the light of the EU’s reform agenda and its possible future developments. The book scrutinizes the legitimacy of the EU’s reform agenda and the ambition to improve the rule of law in investment arbitration. The tensions inherent in the EU’s reform agenda between its own goal of enhancing the rule of law in international investment law, and the practical challenges it faces, form the core of the analysis carried out in this volume.
